cheatSheet:
## NoSQL Databases Cheat Sheet

### Database Types
| Type | Examples | Data Model | Best For |
|------|----------|------------|----------|
| Document | MongoDB, CouchDB | JSON/BSON docs | Flexible schema, CMS, catalogs |
| Key-Value | Redis, DynamoDB | key to value | Caching, sessions, leaderboards |
| Wide-Column | Cassandra, HBase | Row key to columns | Time-series, IoT, analytics |
| Graph | Neo4j, Neptune | Nodes + edges | Social networks, recommendations |

### MongoDB Essentials
| Operation | Command |
|-----------|---------|
| Insert | db.col.insertOne({name: "x"}) |
| Find | db.col.find({age: {: 25}}) |
| Update | db.col.updateOne({_id}, {: {name: "y"}}) |
| Delete | db.col.deleteOne({_id: ObjectId("...")}) |
| Aggregate | db.col.aggregate([{}, {}, {}]) |
| Index | db.col.createIndex({field: 1}) |

### Redis Commands
| Type | Commands |
|------|----------|
| String | GET, SET, INCR, DECR, TTL, EXPIRE |
| Hash | HSET, HGET, HGETALL, HDEL |
| List | LPUSH, RPUSH, LPOP, RPOP, LRANGE |
| Set | SADD, SMEMBERS, SINTER, SUNION |
| Sorted Set | ZADD, ZRANGE, ZRANGEBYSCORE, ZRANK |

### DynamoDB Key Concepts
- Partition Key: Hash for distribution (required)
- Sort Key: Range within partition (optional)
- GSI: Global Secondary Index (different partition key)
- LSI: Local Secondary Index (same partition, different sort)
- RCU/WCU: Read/Write Capacity Units

### CAP Theorem
- CP: MongoDB, Redis, HBase — strong consistency
- AP: Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB — high availability
- Pick 2 of 3: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance

### When SQL vs NoSQL
| SQL | NoSQL |
|-----|-------|
| Complex joins | Denormalized data |
| ACID required | BASE acceptable |
| Fixed schema | Flexible schema |
| Vertical scaling | Horizontal scaling |

plan.overview: Master NoSQL databases for software engineering interviews — covering MongoDB, Redis, DynamoDB, data modeling, and when to choose NoSQL over SQL.
plan.skippedTopics: Couchbase, RavenDB, ArangoDB — rarely tested in interviews. Cassandra covered conceptually only.

sessions.title: NoSQL Fundamentals — Types, CAP Theorem, BASE
sessions.content:
## NoSQL Fundamentals ```mermaid graph TB NoSQL[NoSQL Databases] --> DOC[Document Store
MongoDB, CouchDB] NoSQL --> KV[Key-Value Store
Redis, DynamoDB] NoSQL --> COL[Wide-Column
Cassandra, HBase] NoSQL --> GR[Graph Database
Neo4j, Neptune] DOC -->|JSON docs, nested| D1[Flexible schema
Rich queries] KV -->|key → value| K1[Ultra-fast reads
Caching, sessions] COL -->|row key → columns| C1[Time-series
IoT, analytics] GR -->|nodes + edges| G1[Relationships
Social, fraud] ``` **4 Types:** Document (MongoDB — JSON docs), Key-Value (Redis — hash map), Wide-Column (Cassandra — rows+columns), Graph (Neo4j — nodes+edges). **CAP Theorem:** A distributed system can guarantee only 2 of 3: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance. MongoDB: CP. Cassandra: AP. Redis: CP. **BASE vs ACID:** Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent. NoSQL trades strict ACID for scalability. **When NoSQL:** Flexible/changing schema, horizontal scaling, high write throughput, unstructured data. #### CAP Theorem Diagram ```mermaid graph TB subgraph CAP Theorem C[Consistency
Every read gets latest write] A[Availability
Every request gets response] P[Partition Tolerance
System works despite network splits] end C --- CA[CA: RDBMS
MySQL, PostgreSQL] C --- CP[CP: MongoDB
HBase, Redis] A --- AP[AP: Cassandra
DynamoDB, CouchDB] P --- CP P --- AP ```
objectives: Identify the 4 NoSQL types and their use cases
Explain CAP theorem with real database examples
Contrast BASE with ACID

activities.description: Draw a diagram placing MongoDB, Redis, Cassandra, DynamoDB on the CAP triangle. Justify each placement with one real trade-off.
For each NoSQL type, identify one real-world product that uses it and explain why that type fits the use case.

reviewQuestions: What are the 4 NoSQL types and their primary use cases?:::Document (MongoDB): nested JSON data, flexible schemas, content management. Key-Value (Redis): caching, sessions, leaderboards — O(1) access by key. Wide-Column (Cassandra): time-series, write-heavy workloads, sensor data — optimized for range scans by partition key. Graph (Neo4j): relationship-heavy data, social networks, recommendation engines where JOINs would be expensive.

Explain the CAP theorem and how it applies to MongoDB vs Cassandra.:::CAP states a distributed system cannot simultaneously guarantee Consistency (every read gets the latest write), Availability (every request gets a response), and Partition Tolerance (system continues despite network splits). MongoDB (CP): prioritizes consistency — during a partition, MongoDB may reject writes to maintain a consistent view. Cassandra (AP): prioritizes availability — during a partition, Cassandra serves reads/writes but nodes may have stale data, reconciled later via eventual consistency.

What is BASE and how does it differ from ACID?:::ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) guarantees strict correctness for every transaction — used by relational databases. BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent) relaxes these guarantees for scalability: the system is usually available, state may be temporarily inconsistent, but eventually all nodes converge to the same value. NoSQL systems use BASE to allow horizontal scaling and high availability that strict ACID transactions would prevent.

When should you choose NoSQL over a relational database?:::Choose NoSQL when: (1) Schema is flexible or evolving rapidly; (2) Horizontal scaling is needed — adding more nodes is cheaper than bigger servers; (3) Data is naturally document-shaped (nested JSON) or graph-shaped; (4) Write throughput exceeds what a single RDBMS primary can handle; (5) Specific access patterns benefit from a specialized structure (e.g., Redis for O(1) key lookups, Cassandra for time-series range scans). Choose SQL for complex relationships, multi-table transactions, and when consistency is non-negotiable.
sessions.successCriteria: Can explain the 4 NoSQL types, place real databases on the CAP triangle, and justify NoSQL vs SQL choice for a given scenario.

sessions.title: MongoDB CRUD — insertOne, find, updateOne, deleteOne
objectives: Perform all CRUD operations in MongoDB
Use query operators: $gt, $lt, $in, $exists, $elemMatch
Use update operators: $set, $push, $inc, $pull

activities.description: Create a 'products' collection. Insert 20 documents. Write queries: find by price range, find by tag, find where stock is missing. Update prices for a category. Delete discontinued products.
Implement upsert: use updateOne with {upsert: true} to insert-or-update a user document. Observe the behavior when the document exists vs does not exist.

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between updateOne with $set vs replacing the document?:::updateOne with $set only modifies specified fields, leaving all other fields intact. Example: db.users.updateOne({_id: id}, {$set: {age: 31}}) — only age changes. A full document replacement (db.users.replaceOne) overwrites the entire document with the new document, removing any fields not included. Always use $set for partial updates to avoid accidentally deleting fields.

How does MongoDB handle the _id field?:::Every MongoDB document must have a unique _id field. If you do not provide one on insert, MongoDB automatically generates an ObjectId — a 12-byte BSON type encoding a timestamp, machine ID, process ID, and random increment. This means ObjectIds are roughly time-ordered and unique across a distributed system. You can supply your own _id (string, int, UUID) but it must be unique within the collection. _id is always indexed.

What is the difference between find() and findOne()?:::find() returns a cursor to all matching documents — you iterate over them. It is lazy: documents are fetched in batches as you iterate. findOne() returns the first matching document directly (or null if none), consuming it immediately. Use findOne() when you expect exactly one result (lookup by unique key). Use find() when you expect multiple results. find().limit(1) is equivalent to findOne() but findOne() is more expressive.

What does upsert do in MongoDB updateOne?:::upsert: true tells MongoDB: if no document matches the filter, INSERT a new document using the filter criteria merged with the update. If a matching document exists, update it normally. This is an atomic insert-or-update. Common use case: tracking user sessions — updateOne({userId: id}, {$set: {lastSeen: now}}, {upsert: true}) — creates the session document on first visit, updates it on subsequent visits.
sessions.successCriteria: Can perform all MongoDB CRUD operations, use common query/update operators, and explain _id, upsert, and cursor behavior.

sessions.title: MongoDB Aggregation Pipeline — $match, $group, $sort, $project
objectives: Build multi-stage aggregation pipelines
Use $group with $sum, $avg, $max, $min
Use $lookup for joining collections
Use $unwind to flatten array fields

activities.description: Given an orders collection with embedded items array, build a pipeline: total revenue per customer, average order value per month, top 5 products by quantity sold.
Use $lookup to join orders with a users collection. Project only user name, email, and their total spend. Compare to a two-query approach and discuss trade-offs.

reviewQuestions: Why should $match appear as early as possible in an aggregation pipeline?:::$match placed early reduces the number of documents flowing through subsequent stages. MongoDB can also use indexes to satisfy an early $match, avoiding a full collection scan. If $match comes after $group or $unwind, those stages process ALL documents first — much more expensive. Rule: always $match first to filter, then transform. MongoDB's query optimizer may reorder some stages, but explicit early $match is a best practice.

What is the difference between $group _id: null vs $group _id: '$field'?:::$group _id: '$field' groups documents by that field's value — one output document per unique value (like SQL GROUP BY col). $group _id: null groups ALL documents into a single group — useful for computing grand totals across the entire collection (like SQL aggregate without GROUP BY). Example: {$group: {_id: null, total: {$sum: '$amount'}}} returns one document with the sum of all amounts in the collection.

What does $unwind do and when do you need it?:::$unwind deconstructs an array field — it outputs one document per array element, with all other fields copied from the original. If a document has items: ['A','B','C'], after $unwind you get 3 documents each with a single items value. You need $unwind before $group when you want to aggregate at the element level (e.g., count total quantity per product when products are embedded in order arrays). Without $unwind, $group operates on the whole document, not individual array elements.

How does $lookup work and how does it compare to a SQL JOIN?:::$lookup performs a left outer join to another collection in the same database. It adds an array field to each input document containing matching documents from the foreign collection. Syntax: {$lookup: {from: 'users', localField: 'userId', foreignField: '_id', as: 'user'}}. Like SQL LEFT JOIN, it returns all documents from the primary collection plus matched documents from the secondary (or empty array if no match). Unlike SQL JOINs, $lookup is not as optimized — for high-frequency joins, consider embedding instead.
sessions.successCriteria: Can build aggregation pipelines with $match, $group, $sort, $project, $unwind, and $lookup, and explain performance implications of stage ordering.

sessions.title: MongoDB Indexing & Performance — Compound, Text, explain()
objectives: Create single, compound, and text indexes
Read explain() output to identify COLLSCAN vs IXSCAN
Apply the ESR rule for compound indexes
Use sparse and partial indexes appropriately

activities.description: On a 100k-document orders collection, run explain() on 5 queries before and after adding indexes. Record executionTimeMillis and nScanned for each. Target IXSCAN for all queries.
Design the optimal compound index for this query: db.orders.find({userId:'u1', status:'shipped'}).sort({createdAt:-1}). Apply the ESR rule (Equality, Sort, Range) and verify with explain().

reviewQuestions: What is the ESR rule for compound index design?:::ESR stands for Equality, Sort, Range — the optimal field order in a compound index. Put equality fields (exact match: status: 'active') first, sort fields second, range fields (price: {$gt: 10}) last. This order maximizes index use: equality fields narrow results fastest, sort fields allow index-based sorting (no in-memory sort), range fields are applied last. Example: {userId: 1, createdAt: -1, amount: 1} for find({userId:'x'}).sort({createdAt:-1}).hint({amount:{$gt:50}}).

What is the difference between a sparse index and a partial index?:::A sparse index only includes documents where the indexed field exists (non-null). It skips documents missing the field entirely. A partial index uses a filter expression to index only documents matching a condition — more flexible. Example: sparse index on phoneNumber skips documents without phoneNumber. Partial index with {partialFilterExpression: {status: 'active'}} indexes only active users. Partial indexes are preferred in modern MongoDB (3.2+) because they offer explicit, documented filter logic.

What does COLLSCAN in explain() output mean and how do you fix it?:::COLLSCAN (Collection Scan) means MongoDB read every document in the collection to satisfy the query — O(n) performance. For large collections this is very slow. Fix: create an index on the query filter fields. After adding the index, explain() should show IXSCAN (Index Scan) and nScanned should equal nReturned (only documents that match are scanned). Also check for covered queries where all projected fields are in the index — MongoDB never touches the actual documents.

What is a covered query in MongoDB?:::A covered query is one where all fields in the query predicate AND projection are part of a single index. MongoDB can satisfy the entire query from the index alone without reading any actual documents from disk. This is the fastest possible query execution. To achieve coverage, create a compound index that includes all filter fields and all projected fields. Use explain() and look for 'totalDocsExamined: 0' to confirm a covered query.
sessions.successCriteria: Can design compound indexes using the ESR rule, read explain() output, and identify covered queries.

sessions.title: Redis Data Structures — String, Hash, List, Set, Sorted Set
sessions.content:
## Redis Data Structures

Redis is an in-memory data store. Each data type is purpose-built for specific access patterns.

```bash
# String — cache, counters, flags
SET user:1:name "Alice"
GET user:1:name
INCR page:views          # atomic increment
SETEX session:abc 3600 "data"  # set with TTL (seconds)

# Hash — object fields (like a row)
HSET user:1 name "Alice" age 30 email "a@b.com"
HGET user:1 name
HGETALL user:1

# List — queue/stack (ordered, duplicates ok)
LPUSH jobs "task1" "task2"   # push left
RPOP jobs                     # pop right (queue)
LRANGE jobs 0 -1              # get all

# Set — unique members, O(1) membership check
SADD online:users "u1" "u2" "u3"
SISMEMBER online:users "u1"   # true/false
SINTER online:users premium:users  # intersection

# Sorted Set — ranked leaderboard
ZADD leaderboard 1500 "alice" 2200 "bob"
ZRANGE leaderboard 0 2 WITHSCORES  # top 3
ZRANK leaderboard "alice"           # rank of alice
```
objectives: Use all 5 core Redis data types for appropriate use cases
Explain time complexity of each operation
Choose the right data type for caching, queues, and leaderboards

activities.description: Build a session store using Redis Hashes: store userId, email, role, lastSeen. Set a 30-minute TTL. Implement a function to refresh TTL on each request.
Build a real-time leaderboard using Sorted Sets: add 100 users with scores, retrieve top 10, get rank and score for a specific user, update score with ZINCRBY.

reviewQuestions: When would you use a Redis Hash vs storing a JSON string?:::Use Hash when you need to read or update individual fields frequently — HGET/HSET operate on single fields without deserializing the whole object. Use a JSON string when you always read/write the entire object at once, or when you need to store nested structures (Redis Hashes are flat key-value pairs only). Hash also allows atomic field-level increments (HINCRBY). For most user profile or session data, Hash is more efficient than serializing/deserializing JSON strings.

What is the time complexity of SADD, SISMEMBER, and SINTER?:::SADD (add member): O(N) where N is number of elements added — O(1) for single element. SISMEMBER (check membership): O(1) — constant time lookup, the primary reason to use Sets. SINTER (intersection of sets): O(N*M) where N is the size of the smallest set and M is the number of sets — it iterates the smallest set and checks membership in others. Use SISMEMBER for hot-path membership checks (e.g., is this user banned?). Use SINTER for offline or low-frequency analytics.

What is a Sorted Set and what problems does it solve that a regular Set cannot?:::A Sorted Set stores unique members each associated with a floating-point score. Members are stored in ascending score order. Operations: ZADD (add with score), ZRANGE (get by rank), ZRANGEBYSCORE (get by score range), ZRANK (get rank of member), ZINCRBY (increment score). Solves: leaderboards (rank users by score), rate limiting (score = timestamp, range by time window), priority queues (score = priority), trending content (score = recency + engagement). Regular Sets have no ordering — you cannot ask 'who are the top 10?'

How does Redis handle TTL and what happens when a key expires?:::TTL (Time To Live) is set in seconds with EXPIRE key 3600 or inline with SETEX/SET...EX. Redis uses lazy expiration (checks on access — if expired, deletes and returns nil) plus periodic active expiration (background job samples random keys and deletes expired ones). When a key expires, GET returns nil, EXISTS returns 0. TTL command returns remaining seconds (-1 = no expiry, -2 = key does not exist). Use TTL for: session tokens, cache entries, rate limit windows, one-time tokens.
sessions.successCriteria: Can choose the right Redis data type for a given use case, explain time complexity of key operations, and implement sessions and leaderboards.

sessions.title: Redis Caching Patterns — Cache-aside, Write-through, TTL
sessions.content:
## Redis Caching Patterns

**Cache-aside (Lazy Loading):** App checks cache first; on miss, loads from DB and writes to cache.

```python
import redis, json
r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379)

def get_user(user_id):
    key = f'user:{user_id}'
    cached = r.get(key)
    if cached:
        return json.loads(cached)       # cache hit
    user = db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=%s', user_id)
    r.setex(key, 3600, json.dumps(user))  # cache for 1hr
    return user

# Write-through: update cache on every DB write
def update_user(user_id, data):
    db.execute('UPDATE users SET ...', data)
    r.setex(f'user:{user_id}', 3600, json.dumps(data))
```

**Write-behind (Write-back):** Write to cache only, async flush to DB — highest write throughput, risk of data loss.

**Cache invalidation:** Delete key on update (`r.delete(key)`) or use short TTL.
objectives: Implement cache-aside, write-through, and write-behind patterns
Handle cache invalidation correctly
Choose TTL values based on data freshness requirements

activities.description: Implement cache-aside for a product catalog API. Measure response time with and without cache using 1000 sequential requests. Calculate the cache hit rate after warm-up.
Simulate a cache stampede: 100 concurrent requests for an uncached key. Implement a mutex lock using SET...NX to prevent multiple DB queries on cache miss.

reviewQuestions: What is the cache-aside pattern and what are its trade-offs?:::Cache-aside (lazy loading): on read, check cache first. On hit, return cached value. On miss, query database, store result in cache with TTL, return value. Trade-offs — Pros: only caches data that is actually requested (no wasted memory), cache failure degrades gracefully (app falls back to DB). Cons: first request after cache miss (or TTL expiry) is slow (cache miss penalty), potential for stale data between DB write and next cache population. Most common caching pattern for read-heavy workloads.

What is a cache stampede and how do you prevent it?:::A cache stampede (thundering herd) occurs when a popular cached key expires and many concurrent requests all miss the cache simultaneously, causing all of them to hit the database at once. Prevention strategies: (1) Mutex/lock — use Redis SET key NX EX 10 to acquire a lock; only one request queries DB and populates cache, others wait or return stale data. (2) Probabilistic early expiration — randomly refresh cache before TTL expires. (3) Background refresh — a background job refreshes keys before they expire. (4) Cache warming — pre-populate cache on deployment.

What is the difference between cache invalidation by deletion vs TTL expiry?:::Delete on write (invalidation): when data changes in DB, immediately delete the cache key — next read will miss and re-populate with fresh data. Guarantees consistency but requires coordinating cache deletes with every DB write. TTL expiry: cache automatically expires after a set time. Simpler — no explicit invalidation needed. Allows brief staleness (up to TTL duration). Best for data where slight staleness is acceptable (product catalog, user profiles). Use delete-on-write for financial data or anything requiring immediate consistency.

When would you choose write-through over cache-aside?:::Write-through: every DB write also updates the cache immediately. Use when: read-after-write consistency is critical (user updates their profile and immediately sees the new value), cache miss penalty is unacceptable for new data, and write frequency is not too high (each write hits both DB and cache). Cache-aside is better when writes are frequent and not all written data will be re-read (avoids polluting cache with unread data). Write-through keeps cache always warm but may cache data that is never read again.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement cache-aside and write-through patterns, explain cache stampede prevention, and choose appropriate TTL and invalidation strategies.

sessions.title: DynamoDB — Partition Keys, GSI, LSI, Capacity Modes
sessions.content:
## DynamoDB

DynamoDB is a fully managed AWS key-value and document database. It scales horizontally with no operational overhead.

**Key concepts:**
- **Partition Key (PK):** distributes data across partitions — must be unique for simple PK tables
- **Sort Key (SK):** enables range queries within a partition — (PK+SK) composite key
- **GSI (Global Secondary Index):** alternate PK+SK — query by any attribute
- **LSI (Local Secondary Index):** same PK, alternate SK — must be defined at table creation

```python
import boto3
dynamo = boto3.resource('dynamodb', region_name='us-east-1')
table = dynamo.Table('Orders')

# Put item
table.put_item(Item={'PK':'USER#u1','SK':'ORDER#2024-01','total':99.99,'status':'shipped'})

# Query all orders for a user (single partition scan)
resp = table.query(
    KeyConditionExpression='PK = :pk AND begins_with(SK, :sk)',
    ExpressionAttributeValues={':pk':'USER#u1',':sk':'ORDER#'}
)

# GSI query: find all shipped orders
resp = table.query(
    IndexName='StatusIndex',
    KeyConditionExpression='#s = :s',
    ExpressionAttributeNames={'#s':'status'},
    ExpressionAttributeValues={':s':'shipped'}
)
```

**Capacity modes:** On-demand (pay per request, auto-scales) vs Provisioned (set RCU/WCU, cheaper at steady load).
objectives: Design DynamoDB tables with partition and sort keys
Create and query GSIs and LSIs
Choose between on-demand and provisioned capacity
Apply single-table design principles

activities.description: Design a DynamoDB table for an e-commerce system (users, orders, products) using single-table design. Define PK/SK patterns, GSIs for access patterns: get user, get all orders for user, get orders by status.
Use AWS CLI or SDK to create the table, insert 50 items, and run queries for each access pattern. Measure consumed capacity units for each query.

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between a GSI and an LSI in DynamoDB?:::LSI (Local Secondary Index): same partition key as base table, different sort key. Must be created at table creation — cannot add later. Shares base table's provisioned throughput. Enables range queries on alternate sort attributes within the same partition. GSI (Global Secondary Index): different partition key and optional sort key — can query across all partitions. Can be added to an existing table. Has its own provisioned throughput (or on-demand). More flexible but costs more. LSI for additional sort patterns within a partition; GSI for completely different access patterns.

What is a hot partition in DynamoDB and how do you prevent it?:::A hot partition occurs when too many requests target the same partition key, overwhelming that partition's throughput limits (3000 RCU or 1000 WCU per partition). Common cause: using a low-cardinality partition key like status or date. Prevention: (1) Use high-cardinality partition keys (userId, orderId, UUID). (2) Add random suffix to distribute load: key = userId + '#' + random(1,10). (3) Use write sharding — append a shard suffix and read from all shards. (4) Use DAX (DynamoDB Accelerator) to cache hot reads. On-demand mode also helps by auto-scaling but does not eliminate hot partitions.

What is single-table design in DynamoDB and why is it recommended?:::Single-table design stores multiple entity types in one DynamoDB table, using generic PK/SK patterns to represent different entities and relationships. Example: PK='USER#u1', SK='PROFILE' for user data; PK='USER#u1', SK='ORDER#2024-01' for orders. Benefits: all related data can be fetched in one query (low-latency), no cross-table joins needed, single provisioned capacity pool. Drawbacks: complex access patterns, harder to understand schema, difficult to migrate. Recommended because DynamoDB charges per request — minimizing queries minimizes cost.

When should you choose DynamoDB on-demand vs provisioned capacity?:::On-demand: pay per request, no capacity planning, auto-scales instantly. Choose for: unpredictable or spiky traffic, new applications (unknown load), development/testing, infrequent access. More expensive per request but zero wasted capacity. Provisioned: set RCU/WCU, cheaper per unit at steady load, can use auto-scaling. Choose for: predictable steady-state traffic, cost optimization at high volume, applications where you can forecast load. Rule of thumb: start with on-demand, switch to provisioned once you have 4+ weeks of stable traffic data to right-size capacity.
sessions.successCriteria: Can design a DynamoDB table with appropriate PK/SK patterns and GSIs, query using SDK, and choose between capacity modes.

sessions.title: NoSQL Data Modeling — Embedding vs Referencing, Denormalization
objectives: Apply embedding vs referencing based on access patterns
Design schemas for one-to-many and many-to-many relationships
Explain trade-offs of denormalization in NoSQL

activities.description: Design MongoDB schemas for: (1) Blog posts with comments (1-to-many, bounded), (2) Users with orders (1-to-many, large), (3) Products with categories (many-to-many). Justify embed vs reference for each.
Identify the 3 most common access patterns for a social media feed. Design a MongoDB schema optimized for those patterns. Compare read query count for embedded vs referenced design.

reviewQuestions: What are the criteria for choosing embedding over referencing in MongoDB?:::Embed when: (1) Data is always or almost always accessed together (embed avoids a second query). (2) The embedded array has a bounded, manageable size (MongoDB document limit is 16MB — unbounded arrays will grow forever and hit this). (3) Data is owned by the parent and rarely shared (e.g., order line items owned by an order). (4) Embedded data changes infrequently. Reference when: data is accessed independently, shared across multiple parents (e.g., a category referenced by many products), or when the relationship is many-to-many.

What is the 16MB document size limit in MongoDB and why does it matter for modeling?:::MongoDB documents have a hard 16MB size limit. This limit matters most when embedding arrays that grow over time — e.g., embedding all comments in a post document. If a popular post gets 100k comments, the document eventually hits 16MB and writes fail. Solution: use the bucket pattern (embed batches of N comments, reference to next batch) or reference comments in a separate collection. For one-to-few relationships (orders with 5-10 items), embedding is safe. For one-to-many with unbounded growth, always reference.

What is denormalization in the context of NoSQL and why is it necessary?:::Denormalization means storing redundant copies of data to optimize read performance. In SQL, normalization avoids redundancy. In NoSQL (especially MongoDB and DynamoDB), denormalization is a deliberate design choice: store a copy of frequently-read fields with the document that needs them to avoid joins. Example: embed author name in every post document instead of joining to a users collection — faster reads, but if the author changes their name, you must update all posts. NoSQL lacks join efficiency of SQL — denormalization compensates by pre-joining data at write time.

How do you model a many-to-many relationship in MongoDB?:::Options: (1) Array of references — each document stores an array of related IDs. Example: {_id:'p1', categoryIds:['c1','c2']}. Query: db.products.find({categoryIds:'c1'}). Requires an index on categoryIds for performance. (2) Intermediate junction document — like a SQL join table: {productId:'p1', categoryId:'c1'}. Better for large-scale relationships with additional metadata (e.g., join date, role). (3) Embed one side if one direction is dominant (always query products by category but never categories by product — embed categoryIds in product). Choose based on which direction you query most.
sessions.successCriteria: Can design MongoDB schemas using embedding and referencing appropriately, justify the choice based on access patterns, and avoid unbounded array growth.

sessions.title: NoSQL vs SQL — Trade-offs, When to Use Which
sessions.content:
## NoSQL vs SQL Trade-offs

| Aspect | SQL (RDBMS) | NoSQL |
|---|---|---|
| Schema | Fixed, strict | Flexible, schema-less |
| Scaling | Vertical (bigger server) | Horizontal (more nodes) |
| Transactions | Full ACID | BASE (eventual consistency) |
| Query | SQL joins, complex | Simple by key/index |
| Consistency | Strong | Eventual (configurable) |
| Use case | Complex relations | High throughput, big data |

**Choose SQL when:** complex multi-table relationships, strict ACID transactions (banking, payments), well-defined schema unlikely to change, complex reporting queries.

**Choose NoSQL when:** need horizontal scaling, schema evolves rapidly, data is document/graph-shaped, extreme write throughput (millions/sec), geographically distributed.

**Polyglot persistence:** Use multiple databases — PostgreSQL for transactions, Redis for cache, MongoDB for catalog, DynamoDB for user events.
objectives: Compare SQL and NoSQL across schema, scaling, consistency, and query capabilities
Justify database choice for a given system design scenario
Explain polyglot persistence with real examples

activities.description: System design exercise: you are designing Twitter. Decide which storage technology to use for: user profiles, tweets, timelines, follows graph, DMs, trending topics. Justify each choice.
Given these scenarios, choose SQL or NoSQL and justify: (1) Bank ledger, (2) Product catalog, (3) Real-time chat, (4) Analytics events, (5) Social graph. Discuss with a partner or write explanations.

reviewQuestions: Why does horizontal scaling work better with NoSQL than traditional SQL?:::Traditional RDBMS (PostgreSQL, MySQL) are designed for vertical scaling — adding CPU, RAM, faster disks to a single server. Horizontal scaling (adding more servers) is complex because SQL databases need to maintain ACID guarantees across nodes, requiring distributed transactions and coordination that adds significant overhead. NoSQL databases are designed from the ground up for horizontal scaling: they partition data across nodes (sharding), replicate asynchronously, and relax consistency guarantees (BASE) to avoid the coordination overhead that makes SQL horizontal scaling difficult.

What makes a workload better suited for a document database vs a relational database?:::Document databases excel when: (1) Data has a natural hierarchical/nested structure (a product with variants, prices, and attributes is one document vs 4 normalized tables). (2) Schema varies between records (different product types have different fields — SQL requires nullable columns or EAV anti-pattern). (3) Data is accessed as a unit (read the whole document at once — no joins needed). Relational databases excel when: data has complex many-to-many relationships, multiple entities share data that must stay consistent, and ad-hoc analytical queries need flexible joins.

Explain the CAP theorem implications for choosing between MongoDB and Cassandra.:::MongoDB (CP — Consistency + Partition Tolerance): during a network partition, MongoDB's primary may refuse writes to prevent inconsistency, temporarily sacrificing availability. Best for systems where stale reads are unacceptable (e-commerce inventory, user accounts). Cassandra (AP — Availability + Partition Tolerance): during a partition, Cassandra continues accepting reads and writes on all nodes, but nodes may temporarily have different data — reconciled later via eventual consistency. Best for systems that must stay available under any conditions (messaging, metrics, IoT) where brief inconsistency is tolerable.

What is polyglot persistence and when would you use it?:::Polyglot persistence means using multiple different database technologies within the same application, each chosen for its strengths. Example — e-commerce platform: PostgreSQL for orders and payments (ACID transactions), Redis for product page caching and cart sessions (low-latency reads), Elasticsearch for product search (full-text, faceting), DynamoDB for user behavior events (high write throughput). Trade-off: operational complexity increases — multiple systems to monitor, backup, scale, and integrate. Justified when no single database handles all access patterns efficiently at scale.
sessions.successCriteria: Can compare SQL and NoSQL across key dimensions, recommend the right database for a system design scenario, and explain polyglot persistence.

sessions.title: Interview Patterns & Common NoSQL Questions
sessions.content:
## NoSQL Interview Patterns

Top questions and how to answer them systematically.

**"Design a URL shortener"** — Redis for O(1) key lookup (shortCode → URL), MongoDB for analytics (click counts, geo data).

**"Design a leaderboard"** — Redis Sorted Set: ZADD scores, ZRANGE for top-N, ZRANK for user position. O(log N) updates.

**"How would you cache database results?"** — Cache-aside with Redis: check cache → miss → query DB → SET with TTL. Handle invalidation on writes.

**"MongoDB vs DynamoDB"** — MongoDB: rich queries, aggregation, self-hosted or Atlas. DynamoDB: fully managed, serverless, single-table design, AWS native.

**"What is eventual consistency?"** — Replicas may temporarily diverge after a write; they converge to the same state given time and no new updates. Acceptable for: feeds, recommendations, analytics.
objectives: Answer common NoSQL interview questions with confidence
Apply NoSQL to system design problems
Explain consistency models clearly

activities.description: Mock interview: answer these questions out loud (or in writing) with timing: (1) Design a rate limiter using Redis. (2) How would you store and query a social graph? (3) When would you NOT use NoSQL? (4) How does MongoDB handle transactions? Time yourself: 5 minutes per question.
System design: design a real-time analytics dashboard showing page views per minute for 1M URLs. Choose storage technology, justify schema, estimate read/write throughput, and identify bottlenecks.

reviewQuestions: How would you implement a rate limiter using Redis?:::Use Redis with the sliding window counter pattern. For each user/IP, store a Sorted Set where members are request timestamps: ZADD ratelimit:userId timestamp timestamp. To check rate: ZREMRANGEBYSCORE (remove old entries outside window), ZCARD (count remaining), compare to limit. If under limit, add new entry and allow; if over, reject. Set TTL on key to auto-clean. Alternatively, use a simple counter with INCR and EXPIRE: first request in window sets EXPIRE, subsequent requests INCR. Reject if counter exceeds limit. Token bucket can be implemented with a combination of INCRBY and TTL.

How does MongoDB support multi-document transactions?:::Since MongoDB 4.0, multi-document ACID transactions are supported across multiple documents and collections within a replica set. Since 4.2, across sharded clusters. Syntax mirrors SQL: session.startTransaction(), perform operations, session.commitTransaction() or session.abortTransaction(). Transactions use snapshot isolation. Performance cost: transactions hold locks, generate oplog entries, and add latency — use only when necessary (e.g., transferring credits between two user accounts). For most operations, MongoDB's single-document atomicity (all updates to one document are atomic) is sufficient without transactions.

How would you design a social graph storage? Compare Neo4j vs adjacency list in MongoDB.:::Neo4j (graph database): natively stores nodes (users) and edges (follows/friends) with properties. Graph traversal queries (find friends-of-friends, shortest path) are highly optimized — O(relationships) not O(all users). Best for deep traversals. MongoDB adjacency list: {_id:'u1', follows:['u2','u3']}. Simple for 1-hop queries (who does u1 follow?). Inefficient for multi-hop (who are friends-of-friends? requires multiple queries or $lookup chains). DynamoDB: single-table with PK='USER#u1' SK='FOLLOWS#u2' — efficient for direct follow lookups. Choose Neo4j for recommendation engines; MongoDB/DynamoDB for simple social graphs with shallow traversal.

What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic concurrency control in NoSQL?:::Pessimistic: lock the resource before reading to prevent concurrent modification. Implemented via distributed locks (e.g., Redis SET key NX EX 30 as a lock). Prevents conflicts but reduces throughput — other processes wait. Optimistic: assume conflicts are rare; read freely, include a version field, on write check version still matches. If another process modified the document, reject and retry. MongoDB implements this with findOneAndUpdate + version field check. DynamoDB has built-in optimistic locking via condition expressions: ConditionExpression='version = :v'. Optimistic is faster for low-conflict workloads; pessimistic for high-conflict critical sections.
sessions.successCriteria: Can answer the top 10 NoSQL interview questions, design systems using appropriate NoSQL technologies, and explain consistency and concurrency models.

quizBank.question: Which NoSQL database type is best suited for storing a social network graph where you need to traverse friend-of-friend relationships?
options: Document database (MongoDB)
Key-Value store (Redis)
Graph database (Neo4j)
Wide-Column store (Cassandra)
quizBank.explanation: Graph databases like Neo4j store data as nodes (entities) and edges (relationships) with native graph traversal algorithms. Friend-of-friend queries require multi-hop traversals — graph databases do this in O(relationships) time. Document databases would require multiple queries or expensive $lookup chains for each hop. Neo4j's Cypher query language makes 'find friends of friends who like X' trivial to express and execute efficiently.

quizBank.question: In the CAP theorem, which two properties does MongoDB prioritize?
options: Consistency and Availability
Availability and Partition Tolerance
Consistency and Partition Tolerance
All three equally
quizBank.explanation: MongoDB is a CP system — it prioritizes Consistency and Partition Tolerance. During a network partition, MongoDB's primary may step down and refuse writes rather than allow potentially inconsistent writes. This means MongoDB sacrifices Availability during network splits to maintain a consistent view of data. Cassandra is an AP system, choosing Availability over Consistency. No distributed system can guarantee all three — this is the CAP theorem.

quizBank.question: What is the correct order of stages in a MongoDB aggregation pipeline to optimize performance?
options: $group → $match → $sort → $project
$match → $group → $sort → $project
$project → $match → $group → $sort
$sort → $match → $group → $project
quizBank.explanation: $match should come first to filter documents early using indexes, reducing data flowing through subsequent stages. $group then aggregates the filtered documents. $sort orders the aggregated results. $project reshapes the final output. Placing $match first is critical — it can use indexes and eliminates unnecessary documents before expensive $group operations. Placing $group or $unwind before $match means processing the entire collection before filtering.

quizBank.question: In DynamoDB single-table design, what is the main reason to store multiple entity types in one table?
options: It is required by DynamoDB's data model
To minimize the number of API calls and reduce latency and cost
DynamoDB charges per table, so fewer tables cost less
It makes the schema easier to understand
quizBank.correctAnswer: To minimize the number of API calls and reduce latency and cost
quizBank.explanation: DynamoDB charges per request (on-demand) or per provisioned capacity unit. Each query or GetItem call is a separate request. If users and their orders are in separate tables, fetching a user's profile and last 5 orders requires at minimum 2 API calls. In single-table design, they share the same partition key, and one Query call retrieves both. This reduces latency (fewer round trips) and cost. It is not required by DynamoDB — it is a design pattern optimized for DynamoDB's pricing and performance model.

quizBank.question: Which Redis command would you use to implement an atomic 'check-and-set' distributed lock?
options: SET key value EX 30
SET key value NX EX 30
SETNX key value then EXPIRE key 30
GETSET key value
quizBank.explanation: SET key value NX EX 30 is atomic — it sets the key only if it does not exist (NX = Not eXists) and sets an expiry of 30 seconds in a single command. This is the correct distributed lock pattern: if the key already exists, the command returns nil (lock not acquired). SETNX then EXPIRE is NOT atomic — a process could crash between the two commands, leaving the key without an expiry (permanent lock). The atomic SET...NX EX guarantees the expiry is always set if the lock is acquired.

quizBank.question: What is the ESR rule in MongoDB compound index design?
options: Every Sort must be Reversed
Equality fields first, Sort fields second, Range fields last
Each Schema field requires an index
Embedded documents Sort before References
quizBank.correctAnswer: Equality fields first, Sort fields second, Range fields last
quizBank.explanation: ESR (Equality, Sort, Range) is the optimal field ordering for compound indexes. Equality fields (exact match: status: 'active') come first — they narrow results the most and benefit most from being leftmost in the index. Sort fields come second — they enable index-based sorting without an in-memory sort. Range fields ($gt, $lt, $in with multiple values) come last — they filter but do not enable sorting past this point. Deviating from ESR often results in index-scan + in-memory sort, which is significantly slower.

quizBank.question: What happens to data written to a Cassandra node during a network partition in an AP configuration?
options: Writes are rejected until the partition heals
Writes succeed but are rolled back if not replicated within 30 seconds
Writes succeed on available nodes and sync to others when partition heals
Writes are queued and replayed in order after partition heals
quizBank.correctAnswer: Writes succeed on available nodes and sync to others when partition heals
quizBank.explanation: Cassandra is an AP system — it prioritizes Availability. During a network partition, Cassandra continues accepting writes on whatever nodes are available. These writes are stored locally and, when the partition heals, Cassandra uses anti-entropy (Merkle tree comparison) and hinted handoff to sync diverged data. This means reads during the partition may return stale data (eventual consistency). Cassandra uses 'last write wins' with timestamps to resolve conflicts. This is intentional — Cassandra is designed for systems (metrics, IoT, messaging) where brief inconsistency is acceptable but availability is not negotiable.

quizBank.question: Which embedding pattern should you use when a blog post can have millions of comments?
options: Embed all comments as an array in the post document
Reference comments in a separate collection with a postId field
Use a bucket pattern embedding 100 comments per document
Store comments as a Redis List with the postId as key
quizBank.correctAnswer: Reference comments in a separate collection with a postId field
quizBank.explanation: MongoDB documents have a 16MB size limit. Embedding millions of comments in one document will eventually hit this limit and writes will fail. For unbounded one-to-many relationships, always use referencing: store comments in a separate collection with a postId field and an index on postId. This allows unlimited comments, efficient pagination (skip/limit on comments collection), and independent updates to comments and posts. The bucket pattern is a middle ground for time-series data with high write rates but is more complex.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between a DynamoDB GSI and LSI?
options: GSI can only be created at table creation; LSI can be added later
LSI uses the same partition key as the base table; GSI can use any attribute as partition key
GSI supports strongly consistent reads; LSI only supports eventual consistency
LSI has unlimited throughput; GSI has the same limits as the base table
quizBank.correctAnswer: LSI uses the same partition key as the base table; GSI can use any attribute as partition key
quizBank.explanation: LSI (Local Secondary Index): must use the SAME partition key as the base table, with a different sort key. Can only be created at table creation time. Shares base table throughput. Useful for alternate sort orders within the same partition (e.g., query user's orders by date vs by status). GSI (Global Secondary Index): can use ANY attribute as partition key and sort key. Can be added to an existing table. Has its own separate throughput capacity. Used for completely different query access patterns that span all partitions (e.g., query all orders with status='shipped' regardless of userId).

quizBank.question: What is eventual consistency in the context of a distributed NoSQL database?
options: Data is eventually deleted if not accessed within a TTL window
All replicas will have the same data given enough time with no new updates
Write operations eventually succeed after multiple retries
Consistency is enforced only at the end of each transaction
quizBank.correctAnswer: All replicas will have the same data given enough time with no new updates
quizBank.explanation: Eventual consistency means that if no new updates are made to a data item, all replicas will eventually converge to the same value. Immediately after a write, different replicas may have different values (a reader may get stale data). Given time and a gossip/anti-entropy protocol, all nodes synchronize. This is distinct from strong consistency (every read sees the latest write). Eventual consistency is used by Cassandra, DynamoDB (default), and DNS. It enables higher availability and lower latency by removing the synchronization overhead required for strong consistency.
